Why trust this

Journalism experience. Editorial judgment. News as a public service.

"The best news formats I worked on — at CNN, at Yahoo News Digest — had one thing in common: a real editor making real decisions about what mattered. NTK is that instinct, built into a daily practice."
NTK was built by a former CNN producer and senior editor at Yahoo News Digest, one of the most celebrated mobile news formats of its era. The editorial philosophy draws from Museum Hack's storytelling principles — radical brevity, genuine surprise, layered context. This isn't AI-generated summaries or link aggregation. It's a trained editorial voice making judgment calls, every morning, on your behalf.

Why it's better

Three things no other briefing delivers.

Scrolling isn't reading. Alerts aren't understanding. NTK is built for people who want to actually engage with the world — not just react to it. Reader-funded, which means its only incentive is to be worth your three minutes. Here's what that produces.
I
The pleasure of actually understanding
Print exposure uniquely builds vocabulary, pattern recognition, and verbal fluency in ways that passive media consumption cannot. NTK is designed to feel like reading — and to leave you smarter for it.
→ Cunningham & Stanovich, 1998
II
History as the frame, not the footnote
Every story NTK tells is historicized — placed in the pattern of what came before. The chaos feels senseless because nobody shows you the shape. Every briefing shows you the shape.
→ The Historicizer: context built in
III
Grounded, not sorted
The stream doesn't just overwhelm — it places you in a tribe. NTK's Probabilities and Lies framework surfaces what's distorted and who's distorting it, so you can think clearly across lines.
→ Depolarization built into every story
Who it's for

People who want to read more. Scroll less.

NTK is for people who already know they'd rather be well-informed than well-scrolled — and who just need a product that actually delivers on that in the time they have.

A
The intentional reader
The mom who wants to choose what she reads, not have it chosen for her
She's thoughtful about screen time for her kids. She's less thoughtful about her own. She'd rather spend three intentional minutes on something worth reading than thirty passive minutes on something that makes her feel worse.
B
The commuter
The NYT subscriber who wants real context, not just more content, on the subway
She reads. She listens to podcasts. She's choosing depth over volume already — she just needs the briefing that gives her the frame before she goes deep. NTK is what she reads first.
C
The deliberate professional
The person who deleted the apps and wants something better to replace them
He made the decision to get off the feed. Now there's a gap in the morning. He wants something curated and trustworthy that respects his attention — and doesn't try to colonize it.
How it works

Four sections. Every story. Every morning.

NTK isn't a summary or a link dump. Every story is run through a proprietary editorial framework built to answer the four questions that actually matter — developed over decades of journalism experience at CNN and Yahoo News Digest.
The framework

In 1807, at the close of his presidency, Thomas Jefferson described the ideal newspaper as one that separated its coverage into four distinct sections: Truths, Probabilities, Possibilities, and Lies. No editor built it. NTK did.

Durable · Structural · Historicized
Truth
What is factually established across credible sources — rooted in historical precedent, not in today's narrative. Not opinion. Not framing. What's actually known, and what it means structurally.
I
Near-term · Grounded · Pattern-based
Probabilities
What's likely to happen based on historical patterns, institutional incentives, and political logic. Not predictions — honest, grounded forecasts with the reasoning shown.
II
Black swans · Second-order · Plausible
Possibilities
Lower-probability but plausible outcomes — unintended consequences and edge cases that would surprise most people but not surprise a historian. Every possibility is grounded in something that has actually happened before.
III
Named actors · Traced incentives · Exposed
Lies
False or manipulative narratives in active circulation. NTK names the claim, names who is making it, and traces who benefits. "Some people say" is not NTK's voice.
IV
See it in action

What a story looks like inside NTK.

Every briefing covers 3–4 stories. Each one runs through the same framework. Here's the shape of it — so you know exactly what you're getting.

Today's briefing · Story 01
Story of the day
The Federal Reserve holds rates steady — and what the coverage isn't telling you
Truth
The Fed held the federal funds rate unchanged for the third consecutive meeting, with Chair Powell citing sustained inflation above target and resilient labor data.
Probabilities
One cut before year-end if jobs data softens. Extended hold through 2027 if inflation resurges. Two dissenting votes — the most internal disagreement since 2022 — suggest the next meeting is live.
Possibilities
Markets are pricing in the first scenario. A pivot to the second would be a shock — historically, the Fed's public unanimity has masked internal fractures that only became visible at inflection points.
Lies
"The Fed is caving to political pressure." Powell has not cited political considerations once. The narrative serves those who want a scapegoat for rate policy — and obscures the actual disagreement inside the building.
¶
Three minutes.
One shared reality.
Important stories. Interesting telling. No algorithm deciding what you feel. Every other outlet profits when you stay longer — NTK profits when you understand faster.
